name: Alumina Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Alumina. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Alumina surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption in Alumina with minimal reflection

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution and energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate balancing cleaning efficiency and thermal management

fluenceThreshold.description: Minimum fluence required for effective Alumina surface cleaning

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient material removal with controlled thermal effects

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

power.description: Typical range for ns-pulsed fiber lasers in ceramic cleaning to avoid thermal damage; recommended starting at 100W for light contamination on alumina (high melting point ~2072°C but low thermal conductivity). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics and Coherent for 1064 nm systems; academic paper by Anisimov et al. (2015) in J. Appl. Phys.

scan_speed.description: Effective range for contaminant removal without excessive heat buildup; slower for heavy contamination (500-1000 mm/s), faster for light (3000-5000 mm/s). Recommended 2000 mm/s for optimal balance on alumina. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Association guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime (5-100 ns) preferred for cost-effective cleaning; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-minimal HAZ but less common industrially. 20 ns recommended to limit thermal diffusion in low-conductivity alumina. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Express by Colombier et al. (2012) on ceramic ablation thresholds.

repetition_rate.description: Typical 10-100 kHz for pulsed cleaning; higher rates increase speed but risk heat accumulation on alumina (trade-off: >50 kHz may cause microcracks). 30 kHz recommended for safe operation. Source: Academic literature in Appl. Surf. Sci. by Lorenz et al. (2009) on repetition rate effects in oxide cleaning.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant ablation without substrate damage; alumina damage threshold at 1064 nm ~2-4 J/cm². 1.5 J/cm² recommended for safe cleaning of light contaminants. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. by Autrique et al. (2013) for ceramics at IR wavelengths.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision (50-200 μm for fine details) vs. area coverage (200-500 μm); 200 μm recommended for uniform cleaning on alumina surfaces. Source: Equipment specifications from Trumpf and SPI Lasers for scanning heads in industrial cleaning.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on alumina without risking brittleness-induced damage. Source: Industry practice in case studies from Laser Institute of America reports on ceramic surface preparation.

overlap_ratio.description: 30-80% ensures uniform coverage; 60% recommended to avoid gaps or overexposure on reflective alumina (reflectivity ~20-40% at 1064 nm). Source: Process optimization studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. by Kumar et al. (2018) on scan overlap for laser cleaning.
